1: vayar'eni et Yehoshua hakohen hagadol omed lifnei malakh YHWH vehasatan omed al yemino lesitno: 'Then he showed me Joshua the high priest standing before the angel of the LORD, and Satan standing at his right hand to accuse him.' The fourth vision: the trial of Joshua the high priest. Hasatan (H7854, the adversary, the accuser — with the definite article, indicating a role/title more than a personal name at this stage) stands at the right hand (al yemino) — the position of the prosecuting accuser in a legal proceeding (cf. Psalms 109:6). The legal scene is the heavenly court: Joshua represents the restored post-exilic community standing before YHWH, guilty and accused.
2: vayomer YHWH el hasatan yig'ar YHWH becha hasatan veyig'ar YHWH becha habocher biYerushalayim halo zeh ud mutzal me'esh: 'And the LORD said to Satan: The LORD rebuke you, O Satan! The LORD who has chosen Jerusalem rebuke you! Is not this a brand plucked from the fire?' YHWH's direct rebuke of the Accuser — the divine intervention that terminates the accusation. The image of Joshua as 'a brand plucked from the fire' (ud mutzal me'esh) is the OT's most vivid picture of rescued sinfulness: a burning stick snatched from the flames just before being consumed. The exile was the fire; the return is the rescue. Jude 23 uses the same image ('save others by snatching them out of the fire') for the gospel's rescue mission.
3: veYehoshua hayah lavush begadim tzoa'im ve'omed lifnei hamalakh: 'Now Joshua was standing before the angel, clothed with filthy garments.' Tzoa'im (filthy, H6674 — from tzo'ah, H6674 = excrement, filth) — the strongest word for uncleanness available, the language of human waste. The high priest clothed in excrement represents the post-exilic community's spiritual state: the accumulated guilt of the nation's sin, embodied in its representative before YHWH. The high priestly clothing was meant to represent holiness and purity before YHWH; filthy garments are the exact opposite. This is the human condition before divine grace: standing in one's own defilement before the holy God.
4: vaya'an vayomer el ha'omedim lefanav leimor haseiru habegadim hatzoa'im me'alav vayomer elav re'eh he'evartiy me'aleycha avonecha vehalbeshu etchem machalatzot: 'And he said to those who were standing before him: Remove the filthy garments from him. And to him he said: Behold, I have taken your iniquity away from you, and I will clothe you with pure vestments.' The divine removal of the filthy garments is imputed justification in symbolic action: the iniquity (avon, H5771 — guilt, punishment of sin) is removed (he'evartiy, from avar = to pass over, remove, H5674) and pure vestments (machalatzot, H4254 — festal garments, clean apparel) replace them. This is the OT's most direct symbolic portrayal of the transaction that Paul describes in 2 Corinthians 5:21: God made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God.
5: vaomar yashimu tzanif tahor al rosho vayashimu hatzanif hatahor al rosho vayalbishuhu begadim: 'And I said, Let them put a clean turban on his head. So they put a clean turban on his head and clothed him with garments.' Zechariah's active participation in the vision: the prophet requests the clean turban (tzanif tahor, H4701 — the high priestly head covering described in Exodus 28:36-37, Zechariah 39:30-31, inscribed 'Holy to the LORD'). The turban completes the priestly re-clothing. The scene is the OT's most complete picture of full priestly restoration: filthy garments removed, clean clothing given, clean turban placed. Joshua is re-made as high priest — the representative of the restored people before YHWH.
6: vaya'ad malakh YHWH biYehoshua leimor: 'And the angel of the LORD solemnly assured Joshua.' The Angel of the LORD (divine figure) now charges Joshua with solemn words — the restored high priest receives his commission. The charge is both promise and condition: if Joshua walks in YHWH's ways and keeps his charge, he will have authority in the temple courts and access among those who stand before the heavenly court.
7: koh amar YHWH tzvaot im bidrakhai teleikh ve'im et mishmarti tishhmor vegam atta tidin et beiti vegam tishmor et chatzeirai venatati lecha mahlekhim bein ha'omedim haeleh: 'Thus says the LORD of hosts: If you will walk in my ways and keep my charge, then you shall rule my house and have charge of my courts, and I will give you the right of access among those who are standing here.' The priestly commission's condition: walking in YHWH's ways and keeping his charge. The promised reward: judicial authority in the temple courts (tidin et beiti = you shall judge/rule my house) and access among the heavenly standing ones (ha'omedim = those who stand before YHWH, the angelic council). The high priest, restored and commissioned, is given a place in the heavenly assembly.
8: shema na Yehoshua hakohen hagadol atta vera'eicha hayoshvim lefaneicha ki anshei mofet hemah ki hineni mevi et avdi Tsemach: 'Hear now, O Joshua the high priest, you and your friends who sit before you, for they are men who are a sign: for behold, I will bring my servant the Branch.' The messianic announcement: Tsemach (H6780, the Branch — a royal shoot, a new growth from the Davidic tree). Joshua and his colleagues are anshei mofet (men of sign/portent, H4159 — living symbols, omens of what is coming). They are priestly types of the coming Tsemach: YHWH's servant who will be both priest and king (cf. v13, Zechariah 6:12-13). The Branch is a messianic title used also in Jeremiah 23:5; Zechariah 33:15; Isaiah 4:2; Zechariah 6:12.
9: ki hineh ha'aven asher natati lifnei Yehoshua al even achat shivah einayim hineni mefatteach pituchhah neum YHWH tzvaot umashti et avon ha'aretz hahi beyom echad: 'For behold, on the stone that I have set before Joshua, on a single stone with seven eyes, I will engrave its inscription, declares the LORD of hosts, and I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day.' The stone with seven eyes (even echad shivah einayim) — a single stone engraved by YHWH, with seven eyes (omniscient surveillance), representing either the foundation stone of the temple (the eben shetiyah, the traditional foundation stone) or the person of the Messiah (cf. Isaiah 28:16; Matthew 21:42; 1 Peter 2:6). The removal of iniquity in a single day (beyom echad) is the NT's one-time atonement: the cross as the once-for-all removal of sin (Hebrews 10:10-14).
10: bayom hahu neum YHWH tzvaot tiqre'u ish lere'ehu el tachat gefen veal tachat teenah: 'In that day, declares the LORD of hosts, every one of you will invite his neighbor to come under his vine and under his fig tree.' The eschatological peace formula: sitting under vine and fig tree is the OT's image of agrarian prosperity, security, and covenant blessing (1 Kings 4:25; Micah 4:4). When iniquity is removed (v9), the community experiences the sabbath rest of covenant harmony: neighbor inviting neighbor, shade provided, prosperity shared. The messianic age that follows the day of atonement is the age of community reconciliation.
